sections.header: Verses 1–13 — "Endure Hardship as a Good Soldier"

heb.paragraph: The military metaphor is one of three vocation metaphors: soldier (vv. 3–4), athlete (v. 5), and farmer (v. 6). Each illustrates a different dimension of ministerial endurance: single-minded focus, rule-keeping discipline, and patient labor.

The fourth trustworthy saying introduces a hymnic fragment (vv. 11b–13) structured as four conditional statements. The alternation between faithful human response and faithful divine response creates a covenant-shaped logic.

refs.note: Paul's extended athletic metaphor is the fullest parallel to the athlete image in v. 5.
The 'dying and living with Christ' language echoes Paul's baptismal theology.
Paul's planting/watering imagery complements the farmer metaphor here.

notes.note: Be strong in the grace that is in Christ Jesus. Entrust what you've heard to reliable people who will teach others. This is the apostolic succession of teaching—four generations.
Three pictures of the minister: soldier (focus), athlete (discipline), farmer (patience). Ministry demands all three.
Remember Jesus Christ, risen from the dead, descended from David. Paul suffers to the point of being chained like a criminal. But God's word is not chained.
A trustworthy saying: if we died with him, we live with him; if we endure, we reign. If we disown him, he disowns us. If faithless, he remains faithful—he cannot disown himself.

The soldier must give undivided attention to his commander. The athlete must compete by the rules. The farmer must labor before enjoying the harvest. All three demand present sacrifice for future reward.
The gospel centers on Christ risen and descended from David. Though Paul is chained, the word of God is free. The hymn affirms both the severity and the faithfulness of God.
The four-link chain of transmission: Paul, Timothy, faithful people, others. This is the clearest NT statement of a succession of teaching.
The statement 'he cannot disown himself' grounds God's faithfulness in essential nature rather than human merit.
The 'entrust' is a technical term for the deposit of teaching. The four-generation chain is the Pastorals' clearest articulation of how the tradition survives Paul's death.
The triple metaphor communicates through cumulative effect. The trustworthy saying has a chiastic structure. The final paradox affirms God's faithfulness as ontologically necessary.
The succession instruction creates a pedagogical infrastructure designed to outlast Paul: a succession of pedagogical responsibility, not charismatic authority.

The three vocational metaphors function as cultural scripts accessible to any Greco-Roman audience. The hymnic fragment reframes shame/endurance in eschatological terms. The 'faithless/faithful' couplet is theologically provocative: God's fidelity to his own nature both reassures and warns.

hist.context: Paul's three-metaphor sequence draws on imagery familiar in Greco-Roman moral discourse. Paul Christianizes these by making Christ the commanding officer, rule-setter, and landowner. The hymnic fragment (vv. 11–13) is widely recognized as pre-Pauline, possibly baptismal. The closing paradox—'if we are faithless, he remains faithful'—grounds divine constancy in God's character rather than human performance.

sections.header: Verses 14–26 — "A Worker Who Does Not Need to Be Ashamed"

heb.paragraph: Literally 'to cut straight.' Its metaphorical sense is debated: cutting a straight road, cutting cloth correctly, or handling a text accurately. BDAG gives 'to guide along a straight path.' Paul's point: handle the word of truth competently and accurately.

A medical term for the progressive death of tissue. Paul applies it to the spreading influence of Hymenaeus and Philetus, whose teaching that the resurrection has already occurred was corrupting the faith of some. The metaphor continues the Pastorals' pattern of diagnosing false teaching as disease.

refs.note: Paul's extensive argument against resurrection denial provides the backdrop for understanding the Hymenaeus/Philetus heresy.
The eschatological tension between 'already died with Christ' and 'not yet raised in glory' is precisely what the over-realized position disturbs.
The 'firm foundation of God' draws on the Isaianic foundation-stone tradition.

notes.note: Warn against quarreling about words. Present yourself to God as one approved, a worker who does not need to be ashamed, rightly handling the word of truth.
Avoid irreverent babble. Hymenaeus and Philetus have wandered from truth by teaching the resurrection has already occurred, destroying the faith of some.
God's solid foundation stands firm. In a large house there are noble and ignoble vessels. Those who cleanse themselves will be instruments for special purposes.
Flee evil desires. Pursue righteousness, faith, love, peace. The Lord's servant must be kind, able to teach, patient, gently instructing opponents in the hope God will grant repentance.
The worker who rightly handles truth contrasts with quarrelers about words. Hymenaeus and Philetus destroyed faith by teaching a spiritualized resurrection. Yet God's foundation remains firm.

The vessel metaphor teaches that personal purity determines usefulness. The Lord's servant must not be quarrelsome but gentle, patient, instructive—even toward opponents. Gentleness is the minister's most powerful weapon.
The verb 'rightly handling' occurs only here in the NT. The context suggests accurate, faithful exposition of truth.
The teaching that the resurrection has already occurred represents an over-realized eschatology that spiritualized future bodily resurrection.
The unashamed worker who rightly handles the word contrasts with Hymenaeus and Philetus. The gangrene metaphor diagnoses this as a spreading pathology, not a contained error.

The vessel metaphor introduces self-purification: usefulness depends on the vessel's cleanliness. The counsel culminates in a striking point: God may grant repentance to opponents, indicating that turning from error is a divine gift.

The rightly-handling charge constructs Timothy as the competent teacher against incompetent opponents. The over-realized eschatology collapses the tension that sustains Christian ethics: if the resurrection is past, the moral imperative loses its ultimate grounding.

The pastoral advice in vv. 24–26 is remarkably restrained: the Lord's servant engages opponents with gentleness, and the outcome is left to divine sovereignty. Pastoral wisdom: firm in truth, gentle in manner, hopeful in expectation.

hist.context: Hymenaeus (previously in 1 Tim 1:20) and Philetus taught that the resurrection had already occurred—an over-realized eschatology that spiritualized bodily resurrection. The vessel metaphor (vv. 20–26) addresses personal purity as a condition for usefulness, culminating in gentle pastoral counsel toward opponents.

themes.note: Faith peaks through the endurance theme and four-generation transmission. Mission drives the charge to teach and correct opponents. Holiness emerges in the vessel metaphor.

rows.label: vv.1–2
rows.text: Entrust the deposit to faithful teachers

rows.label: vv.3–13
rows.text: Three metaphors + trustworthy saying on endurance

lit.note: Chapter 2 addresses faithful transmission under pressure. Succession (vv. 1–2), vocational metaphors (vv. 3–13), approved-worker charge (vv. 14–19), and vessel parable (vv. 20–26) all ask: how does the gospel survive Paul’s death?

hebtext.note: The military metaphor is one of three vocation metaphors: soldier (vv. 3–4), athlete (v. 5), and farmer (v. 6). Each illustrates a different dimension of ministerial endurance: single-minded focus, rule-keeping discipline, and patient labor.

The fourth trustworthy saying introduces a hymnic fragment (vv. 11b–13) structured as four conditional statements. The alternation between faithful human response and faithful divine response creates a covenant-shaped logic.

Literally 'to cut straight.' Its metaphorical sense is debated: cutting a straight road, cutting cloth correctly, or handling a text accurately. BDAG gives 'to guide along a straight path.' Paul's point: handle the word of truth competently and accurately.

A medical term for the progressive death of tissue. Paul applies it to the spreading influence of Hymenaeus and Philetus, whose teaching that the resurrection has already occurred was corrupting the faith of some. The metaphor continues the Pastorals' pattern of diagnosing false teaching as disease.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Timothy
ppl.role: Paul’s young protégé; co-author of six NT letters
ppl.text: Timothy is recruited at Lystra on the second journey (Acts 16:1–3) — the son of a Jewish mother and Greek father, circumcised by Paul to aid Jewish outreach. He becomes Paul’s most trusted emissary, sent to troubled churches when Paul cannot go. Paul twice calls him "my true son in the faith" and addresses two personal letters to him. He is co-author of six Pauline letters and appears at the end of Hebrews.

ppl.name: Hymenaeus
ppl.text: Hymenaeus (previously in 1 Tim 1:20) and Philetus taught that the resurrection had already occurred—an over-realized eschatology that spiritualized bodily resurrection…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Endurance and the Approved Worker

translations.text: Similarly, anyone who competes as an athlete does not receive the victor’s crown except by competing according to the rules.
An athlete is not crowned unless he competes according to the rules.
for which I am suffering even to the point of being chained like a criminal. But God’s word is not chained.
for which I am suffering, bound with chains as a criminal. But the word of God is not bound!

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "Be strong in the grace that is in Christ Jesus. Entrust what you've heard to reliable people who will teach others. This is the apostolic succession of teaching—four generations."

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "The soldier must give undivided attention to his commander. The athlete must compete by the rules. The farmer must labor before enjoying the harvest. All three demand present sacrifice for future rewa…"

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The four-link chain of transmission: Paul, Timothy, faithful people, others. This is the clearest NT statement of a succession of teaching."

thread.text: Paul's extended athletic metaphor is the fullest parallel to the athlete image in v. 5.
The 'dying and living with Christ' language echoes Paul's baptismal theology.
Paul's planting/watering imagery complements the farmer metaphor here.
Paul's extensive argument against resurrection denial provides the backdrop for understanding the Hymenaeus/Philetus heresy.
The eschatological tension between 'already died with Christ' and 'not yet raised in glory' is precisely what the over-realized position disturbs.
The 'firm foundation of God' draws on the Isaianic foundation-stone tradition.

coaching.tip: Soldier, athlete, farmer (vv. 3-6)—three images of disciplined endurance. Soldiers don't entangle themselves in civilian affairs; athletes compete by the rules; farmers labor before harvest. Ministry requires single-mindedness, discipline, and patience.

'A worker who does not need to be ashamed, rightly handling the word of truth' (v. 15). 'Rightly handling' (orthotomounta) means cutting straight. Careful interpretation isn't optional; error damages souls. Precision matters in proclamation.
